frame_analyses.description: First instrument enters the field and task timing begins as the trainee reaches for the needle and suture.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Controlled entry, but there is a short setup interval before the needle is fully oriented.

frame_analyses.description: The needle is being reloaded and aligned for the initial bite through the Penrose.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle control is steady; orientation is acceptable but not especially fast.

frame_analyses.description: A bite is passed through one marked target on the slit Penrose and the needle is advanced across the opening.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Tissue handling remains gentle and the drain is not avulsed during passage.

frame_analyses.description: The first knot throw is being formed at the approximation site with two wraps visible before the throw is seated.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: This is consistent with a surgeon's knot; right-hand lead is the best estimate.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is underway with the opposite hand appearing to lead as the knot is squared down.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Alternation appears correct, though several regrasping moves add time before the throw is fully seated.

frame_analyses.description: The third scored throw is formed and tightened down onto the previous throws.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: This looks like a single throw and appears to alternate back to the right hand.

frame_analyses.description: After the third required throw, the trainee appears to place an additional reinforcing throw before proceeding to cut.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Likely improves security, but it increases total completion time.

frame_analyses.description: Scissors are introduced and one suture end is being trimmed inside the trainer.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting is deliberate but prolonged, with multiple small positioning adjustments near the knot.

frame_analyses.description: Final trimming is completed and the instruments withdraw from the field.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The task appears finished with the knot intact and the drain still secured to the block.

knot_assessments.notes: Double wrap is visible during formation and the right instrument appears to lead the first throw.
Single throw appears left-hand led and alternates appropriately from throw 1, though seating is slowed by regrasping.
Single throw appears right-hand led and alternates appropriately from throw 2; an extra reinforcing throw seems to be added afterward before cutting.

technique_summary: The trainee handled the Penrose gently and achieved a stable final closure without avulsion or an obvious residual slit gap. Needle placement was close to the marks and the three scored throws appear to alternate right-left-right, but repeated regrasping and an additional reinforcing throw before cutting made the performance substantially slower than necessary. The task was not slow because of a major error; it was slow because of accumulated small inefficiencies, especially during knot tying and trimming.
improvement_suggestions: Reduce regrasping between the second and third throws; a large portion of the total time is spent reorienting strands instead of advancing the knot.
Keep loop sizes smaller and closer to the knot so each single throw can be laid down and squared more efficiently.
Once the third throw is flat and secure, transition directly to cutting rather than adding a prolonged extra reinforcing throw unless the knot clearly looks unstable.
strengths: Needle bites were placed close to the marked targets and the drain remained attached to the block throughout the task.
Final knot approximation looked secure with no obvious visible gap after tightening, and required hand alternation appears to have been maintained.
Tissue handling was gentle throughout with no rough traction that threatened drain avulsion.

rubric_assessment.overall_judgment: Technically acceptable but time-inefficient. Strongest parts were gentle tissue handling, maintenance of drain fixation, and a final closure that looked secure without an obvious persistent slit gap. The biggest limitation was efficiency: repeated regrasping during knot construction, long loop management, and an extra reinforcing throw after the three required scored throws likely improved subjective security but cost a substantial amount of time. Safe and serviceable performance pulled down by inefficiency rather than major technical failure.
biggest_time_losses: Large loops rather than compact throws close to the target
Slow transition from third required throw into trimming
Extra reinforcing throw(s) after the required knot sequence
Deliberate but prolonged scissor positioning before final cut completion
rubric_assessment.uncertainty_notes: Moderate overall confidence. Most confident about absence of avulsion/gap and overall time inefficiency. Less confident about exact mm deviation and the lead-hand call on the third throw.
